planting.note: Plant divisions in early spring before new growth emerges, setting roots at their original depth in well-drained, humus-rich soil. Avoid disturbing established plants unnecessarily, as Spiranthes lacera resents frequent division.

directions: Plant slender ladies' tresses in a naturalistic setting with dappled light, such as the edge of a woodland or an open meadow, in well-drained loamy or sandy soil with moderate organic matter. Set divisions shallowly at the depth they were growing, water in gently, and mulch lightly with leaf litter to mimic the forest-floor conditions this orchid prefers. Once established, it requires little supplemental water and benefits from minimal fertilization, which can encourage weedy competition. Allow the basal rosette to die back naturally each autumn and leave surrounding leaf litter in place to support the mycorrhizal fungi essential to its survival.

planting.note: Best established in spring from nursery-grown divisions into dry, open sites with sandy or gravelly, low-fertility soil. Avoid clay and consistently moist soils, which promote root rot.

directions: Choose a sunny spot with dry, infertile, well-draining sandy or rocky soil — conditions that would starve most garden plants but suit this diminutive orchid perfectly. Loosen the planting area to about six inches deep, mix in a small amount of coarse sand if drainage is questionable, and set the tuberous roots at their original depth. Water lightly after planting and then rely almost entirely on rainfall once the plant has settled in; overwatering is the most common cause of failure. Because of its tiny stature, plant little ladies' tresses in small drifts of three or more and mark the site, as the foliage disappears after flowering and the plant is easy to accidentally disturb.
sources: North Carolina Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox

planting.note: Transplant divisions in early spring into consistently moist, open ground with full sun to light shade. Incorporating organic matter improves establishment in heavier soils, but avoid excessive fertility.

directions: Site spring ladies' tresses in a moist meadow, rain garden, or roadside planting with full sun to partial shade and consistently moist, humus-enriched soil that does not dry out in summer. Prepare the planting area by removing competing sod and loosening the soil to allow the fleshy roots to establish without obstruction. Plant divisions at their original depth, firm the soil around the roots, and water thoroughly; continue to keep the soil evenly moist through the first growing season. This orchid blooms in spring rather than fall, making it an excellent companion to other early-season natives, and it will slowly colonize suitable areas once the mycorrhizal community is established.

planting.note: Plant in early spring into cool, moist, rich woodland soil with deep shade and excellent drainage. Success depends heavily on intact mycorrhizal associations; source only from reputable nurseries that propagate this species ethically.

directions: Hooker's orchid demands the specific conditions of a cool, shaded northern hardwood or mixed forest understory — rich, moist, well-drained loam with a deep leaf-litter layer and established fungal networks in the soil. Prepare a planting pocket by blending existing woodland soil with well-aged leaf compost, and set the roots at their original depth without burying the crown. Keep the site consistently moist but never waterlogged, and refresh the leaf mulch each autumn to sustain the mycorrhizal partners this orchid relies on entirely for nutrients. Do not fertilize, transplant repeatedly, or remove the surrounding tree canopy, as this rare orchid is extremely sensitive to any disruption of its cool, stable woodland microclimate.

planting.note: Establish divisions in early spring into permanently moist or wet, nutrient-poor to moderately fertile soil in partial shade or open sun. Avoid rich garden soils, which encourage lush foliage at the expense of flowering.

directions: Plant southern marsh orchid in a rain garden, bog margin, or consistently moist border where the soil stays wet through the growing season; the tuberous roots tolerate standing water in winter but prefer slowly draining, moisture-retentive soil in summer. Amend heavy clay with coarse grit to prevent anaerobic conditions while maintaining the high moisture this species requires, and incorporate a modest amount of leaf mold to improve structure. Set divisions at their original depth in early spring, water generously to eliminate air pockets, and top-dress lightly with a grit-and-leaf-mold mix each autumn. Once established, clumps can be divided every three to four years in early autumn after flowering and the divisions used to create naturalistic drifts in wet meadow or pondside plantings.
